1: Optimizing SCND with carbon-rich hydrolysates from typical organic wastes: Material composition, augmentation performance, microbiome response, and life cycle impact
Zhendong Yang a, , Zhaoyue Yang Yazhi Zhan Cheng Hu Zhenyu Zhang a , Miao He Jin Huang a, , Jing Wang a,b , Huaqun Yin Zhenghua Liu c,'

<list>a School of Architecture and Civil Engineering, Chengdu University, Chengdu, 610106, Sichuan, China 
b Sichuan Provincial Engineering Research Center of City Solid Waste Energy and Building Materials Conversion and Utilization Technology, Chengdu, 610106, Sichuan, China 
C School of Minerals Processing and Bioengineering, Central South University, Changsha, 410083, Hunan, China</list>

ARTICLE INFO|ABSTRACT Keywords: Organic waste hydorlysate SCND Nitrogen depletion Microbial community Life cycle assessment|The rapid growth of production and consumption has led to severe environmental pollution, creating a major challenge to achieving the United Nations' sustainable development goals (SDGs). To address it, recycling of organic wastes into value-added products is a possible solution. In this work, four typical organic wastes including sewage sludge (SS), chicken manure (CM), food waste (FW), and corn straw (CS) were employed to produce hydrolysates augmenting shortcut nitrification-denitrification (SCND) for nitrogen depletion in waste- water. The hydrolysates were carbon-rich, with total COD (TCOD), soluble COD (SCOD), and volatile fatty acids (VFA) concentrations ranging from 32.5 to 102.7, 5.7 to 48.4, and 2.0-16.5 mg/L, respectively. The most effective nitrogen depletion was obtained in units supplemented with CM and FW hydrolysates, which had reduced average NH3-N concentrations and near-zero TN removal failure rates under legal requirements. The microbial community analysis demonstrated that various functional bacteria from phylum to genus level were detected in all scenarios, which was corroborated by abundant genetic functions involved in nitrogen meta- bolism. Further, life cycle assessment revealed negative environmental impact on all categories, with an exception of eutrophication potential (EP) with negative values (~-0.04 kg Phosphate eq.), allowing positive net environmental benefit (NEB). Operational cost analysis revealed that CM and FW are more effective but costlier than SS and CS. Together, these results indicate that, after hydrolysis, organic wastes can be efficient stimulant augmenting SCND performance for nitrogen depletion in wastewater, benefiting the overall environmental impact.
2: The rapid growth of production and consumption, which has led to severe environmental pollution and exceeded the environment's ca- pacity to sustainably support natural resources, is creating a major challenge to achieving the United Nations' sustainable development goals (SDGs) (Guang-Wen et al., 2022; Abbass et al., 2022). This has resulted in issues such as climate change, global warming, land degra- dation, loss of biocapacity, and various pollution-related problems. To combat these issues, establishing environmental sustainability is crucial in terms of balancing the economic and environment benefits (Amin

et al., 2022). In this context, almost all global economies signed the 26th Conference of Parties (COP26), pledging to adopt a sustainable devel- opment future to combat severe environmental changes, particularly global warming (Murshed et al., 2022; Suki et al., 2022). Recycling plays a crucial role in achieving these goals, helping to conserve natural re- sources, reduce energy consumption, and lower greenhouse gas emis- sions (Cheng et al., 2022). According to a World Bank report, if the world can increase its recycling rate to 70%, it could reduce global greenhouse gas emissions by 20% (Ellis, 2018). Hereafter, resource recycling and waste minimizing have been proposed as one of the most effective approaches to boost progress toward achieving SDGs, partic- ularly SDG12 (Responsible Consumption and Production), SDG13

(Climate Action), and SDG15 (Life on Land) (Sharma et al., 2020; Usmani et al., 2021; Cheng et al., 2023).

Organic waste is an important resource that is generated as a result of anthropogenic activities and can be recycled. It is typically classified into two categories: i) urban organic waste (e.g., food waste, sewage sludge, and rubber residues) associated with residents' daily lives and industrial production (Federico et al., 2022); ii) agricultural organic waste connected to livestock or crop cultivation (e.g. animal manure, crop straws, and leaves) (Aravani et al., 2022). Around 2 billion metric tons of organic waste are collected globally each year, and this figure is expected to exceed 3 billion metric tons per year by 2050 (Ellis, 2018). Improper disposal of organic waste leads to significant environmental damage and results in the loss of valuable nutrients and carbon sources. Recycling organic waste provides a solution for its more appropriate management, making secondary raw materials available on the market, and fostering a circular economy that closes the loop from waste reduction to environmental protection (Sreekala et al., 2022). This turns organic waste into valuable resources, reduces carbon footprints, de- creases land usage and environmental pollution, and thus contributes to sustainable development and achieving the SDGs.

On the other hand, wastewater treatment aimed at nitrogen deple- tion often lacks a carbon source. Nitrogen depletion involves the nitri- fication and denitrification bio-processes, where NH3-N is converted to NOx-N by nitrification and NOx-N is transformed to N2 and released into the atmosphere by denitrification. This process is energy and carbon- intensive, typically requiring a C/N ratio greater than 10 (Kosgey et al., 2022). Intermittent aeration was used to implement a nitrite shortcut procedure, also known as shortcut initrification-denitrification (SCND), in order to reduce the carbon intensity. This was achieved by leveraging the sensitivity difference to the dissolved oxygen level of nitrifiers and denitrifiers. By controlling parameters such as pH, ORP, and DO, carbon consumption can be significantly reduced (up to 40%) (Zhao et al., 2022). However, in most cases, the C/N ratio is not efficient enough to support effective nitrogen depletion in wastewater, which has been noted by Li et al. (2022). To enhance the carbon content and stimulate SCND, supplementing organic waste is necessary. This can lead to a win-win situation where expensive commercial carbon sources (such as lactate, glucose, and ethanol) can be replaced with organic waste, thus reducing material costs. Moreover, the organic waste can be successfully managed with complete recycling of valuable components. Nevertheless, it's important to note that organic waste is primarily composed of macromolecular substances, such as cellulose and lignin, that are linked by glycosidic and peptide bonds, which are difficult for microbes to utilize. This leads to low nitrogen removal efficiency.

Under this background, the hydrolysis of organic wastes is neces- sarily proposed to heterogenize the organic wastes to low-weight mo- lecular compounds that are more easily accessible to microbial metabolism, particularly volatile fatty acids (VFA). These compounds not only provide energy and raw materials for cellular assembly but also

promote synergistic interactions among microbial communities, such as biofilm formation, cell movement, and signal transduction (Pandey et al., 2022; Sivaram et al., 2019). Various studies have successfully applied different organic waste hydrolysates to enhance biological ni- trogen depletion and compared their effects to commercial carbons. For example, Yang et al. (2019) reported that pig manure hydrolysate can effectively improve the total nitrogen (TN) removal efficiency to over 90% at an optimum C/N ratio of 6.5, which is even better than using sodium lactate. Liu et al. (2022) hydrolyzed activated sludge after fermentation to yield a high-quality internal carbon source for waste- water treatment, achieving a nitrogen removal rate of up to 98.5% that was comparable to sodium lactate. Zhang et al. (2022) found that food waste hydrolysate increased microbial selectivity to functional species related to nitrogen removal compared to glucose. However, there is a lack of information on the effectiveness of hydrolysates in the SCND process in terms of treatment performance and material savings. Therefore, it's necessary to select the optimum organic waste materials cost-effectively and compare their performance with different hydroly- sates. Moreover, although using organic waste hydrolysates may have lower material costs and a shorter conversion pathway, processing organic wastes, including transportation, storage, homogenization, and hydrolysis, is material and energy-consuming. Thus, using organic waste hydrolysates may inevitably result in significant environmental impacts due to operating and inventory input. Whether using hydrolysates pro- vides net economic and ecological benefits is still unknown, SO a trade-off balance should be evaluated.

To address the gaps, this study examines the potential of using typical organic wastes, including sewage sludge (SS), chicken manure (CM), food waste (FW), and corn straw (CS), to produce hydrolysates for nitrogen depletion in an SCND wastewater treatment system. By combining (bio)chemical analysis and life cycle assessment, we aim to answer questions about the composition of raw materials and hydroly- sates, augmentation performances, and environmental benefits compared to the process's environmental burden. This study may pro- vide detailed and relevant data with internal validity, enabling gener- alization to future applications with similar technological schemes. Specifically, the results are expected to offer operational parameters, characteristic data, and a roadmap for managing typical organic waste to enhance wastewater treatment. In this way, the amount of organic waste that otherwise ended up in landfills can be reduced, where it can generate methane and other greenhouse gases that contribute to climate change; the organic wastes are recycled in a meaningful way reducing the nitrogen in wastewater to meet legal requirements and prevent eutrophication in receiving water bodies. We anticipate that our find- ings will have implications for waste management policymakers, engi- neers, and practical scientists who are striving to achieve a more circular economy with minimal waste and efficient resource utilization.

6: The rapid growth of production and consumption, which has led to severe environmental pollution and exceeded the environment's ca- pacity to sustainably support natural resources, is creating a major challenge to achieving the United Nations' sustainable development goals (SDGs) (Guang-Wen et al., 2022; Abbass et al., 2022). This has resulted in issues such as climate change, global warming, land degra- dation, loss of biocapacity, and various pollution-related problems. To combat these issues, establishing environmental sustainability is crucial in terms of balancing the economic and environment benefits (Amin

et al., 2022). In this context, almost all global economies signed the 26th Conference of Parties (COP26), pledging to adopt a sustainable devel- opment future to combat severe environmental changes, particularly global warming (Murshed et al., 2022; Suki et al., 2022). Recycling plays a crucial role in achieving these goals, helping to conserve natural re- sources, reduce energy consumption, and lower greenhouse gas emis- sions (Cheng et al., 2022). According to a World Bank report, if the world can increase its recycling rate to 70%, it could reduce global greenhouse gas emissions by 20% (Ellis, 2018). Hereafter, resource recycling and waste minimizing have been proposed as one of the most effective approaches to boost progress toward achieving SDGs, partic- ularly SDG12 (Responsible Consumption and Production), SDG13

(Climate Action), and SDG15 (Life on Land) (Sharma et al., 2020; Usmani et al., 2021; Cheng et al., 2023).

Organic waste is an important resource that is generated as a result of anthropogenic activities and can be recycled. It is typically classified into two categories: i) urban organic waste (e.g., food waste, sewage sludge, and rubber residues) associated with residents' daily lives and industrial production (Federico et al., 2022); ii) agricultural organic waste connected to livestock or crop cultivation (e.g. animal manure, crop straws, and leaves) (Aravani et al., 2022). Around 2 billion metric tons of organic waste are collected globally each year, and this figure is expected to exceed 3 billion metric tons per year by 2050 (Ellis, 2018). Improper disposal of organic waste leads to significant environmental damage and results in the loss of valuable nutrients and carbon sources. Recycling organic waste provides a solution for its more appropriate management, making secondary raw materials available on the market, and fostering a circular economy that closes the loop from waste reduction to environmental protection (Sreekala et al., 2022). This turns organic waste into valuable resources, reduces carbon footprints, de- creases land usage and environmental pollution, and thus contributes to sustainable development and achieving the SDGs.

On the other hand, wastewater treatment aimed at nitrogen deple- tion often lacks a carbon source. Nitrogen depletion involves the nitri- fication and denitrification bio-processes, where NH3-N is converted to NOx-N by nitrification and NOx-N is transformed to N2 and released into the atmosphere by denitrification. This process is energy and carbon- intensive, typically requiring a C/N ratio greater than 10 (Kosgey et al., 2022). Intermittent aeration was used to implement a nitrite shortcut procedure, also known as shortcut initrification-denitrification (SCND), in order to reduce the carbon intensity. This was achieved by leveraging the sensitivity difference to the dissolved oxygen level of nitrifiers and denitrifiers. By controlling parameters such as pH, ORP, and DO, carbon consumption can be significantly reduced (up to 40%) (Zhao et al., 2022). However, in most cases, the C/N ratio is not efficient enough to support effective nitrogen depletion in wastewater, which has been noted by Li et al. (2022). To enhance the carbon content and stimulate SCND, supplementing organic waste is necessary. This can lead to a win-win situation where expensive commercial carbon sources (such as lactate, glucose, and ethanol) can be replaced with organic waste, thus reducing material costs. Moreover, the organic waste can be successfully managed with complete recycling of valuable components. Nevertheless, it's important to note that organic waste is primarily composed of macromolecular substances, such as cellulose and lignin, that are linked by glycosidic and peptide bonds, which are difficult for microbes to utilize. This leads to low nitrogen removal efficiency.

Under this background, the hydrolysis of organic wastes is neces- sarily proposed to heterogenize the organic wastes to low-weight mo- lecular compounds that are more easily accessible to microbial metabolism, particularly volatile fatty acids (VFA). These compounds not only provide energy and raw materials for cellular assembly but also

promote synergistic interactions among microbial communities, such as biofilm formation, cell movement, and signal transduction (Pandey et al., 2022; Sivaram et al., 2019). Various studies have successfully applied different organic waste hydrolysates to enhance biological ni- trogen depletion and compared their effects to commercial carbons. For example, Yang et al. (2019) reported that pig manure hydrolysate can effectively improve the total nitrogen (TN) removal efficiency to over 90% at an optimum C/N ratio of 6.5, which is even better than using sodium lactate. Liu et al. (2022) hydrolyzed activated sludge after fermentation to yield a high-quality internal carbon source for waste- water treatment, achieving a nitrogen removal rate of up to 98.5% that was comparable to sodium lactate. Zhang et al. (2022) found that food waste hydrolysate increased microbial selectivity to functional species related to nitrogen removal compared to glucose. However, there is a lack of information on the effectiveness of hydrolysates in the SCND process in terms of treatment performance and material savings. Therefore, it's necessary to select the optimum organic waste materials cost-effectively and compare their performance with different hydroly- sates. Moreover, although using organic waste hydrolysates may have lower material costs and a shorter conversion pathway, processing organic wastes, including transportation, storage, homogenization, and hydrolysis, is material and energy-consuming. Thus, using organic waste hydrolysates may inevitably result in significant environmental impacts due to operating and inventory input. Whether using hydrolysates pro- vides net economic and ecological benefits is still unknown, SO a trade-off balance should be evaluated.

To address the gaps, this study examines the potential of using typical organic wastes, including sewage sludge (SS), chicken manure (CM), food waste (FW), and corn straw (CS), to produce hydrolysates for nitrogen depletion in an SCND wastewater treatment system. By combining (bio)chemical analysis and life cycle assessment, we aim to answer questions about the composition of raw materials and hydroly- sates, augmentation performances, and environmental benefits compared to the process's environmental burden. This study may pro- vide detailed and relevant data with internal validity, enabling gener- alization to future applications with similar technological schemes. Specifically, the results are expected to offer operational parameters, characteristic data, and a roadmap for managing typical organic waste to enhance wastewater treatment. In this way, the amount of organic waste that otherwise ended up in landfills can be reduced, where it can generate methane and other greenhouse gases that contribute to climate change; the organic wastes are recycled in a meaningful way reducing the nitrogen in wastewater to meet legal requirements and prevent eutrophication in receiving water bodies. We anticipate that our find- ings will have implications for waste management policymakers, engi- neers, and practical scientists who are striving to achieve a more circular economy with minimal waste and efficient resource utilization.

10: Investigations were carried out in reactors set up in a solid waste products recycling plant in Southwest China (Xinxin Environment Ltd.). The organic waste, including SS, CM, FW, and CS, was first processed before being subjected to hydrolysis. The impact of the resulting hy- drolysates on nitrogen depletion was further performed in the sequential batch reactor (SBR) based in the inner wastewater treatment station of the plant. The wastewater was directly collected from the inlet of the station with a low C/N ratio (112.18 + 16.27 mg /69.1 + 4.9 mg TN.L-1. SS was collected at the treatment station's dewatering workshop; CM was collected from a chicken farm; FW (solid phase) was collected from a full-scale food waste treatment facility after selection and liquid/solid separation; CS was collected from the storage field of the nearby village.

The full processes were briefly described as follows: Four process lines representing SS, CM, FW, and CS scenarios were set up, respec- tively. The raw material was initially transported to pretreatment in each scenario. SS and CM were prepared by beads milling (magnetic stirring with grinding beads), whereas FW and CM were processed by high-pressure homogenization. The pretreated raw material was sub- jected to thermo-alkaline hydrolysis in a magnetically driven reactor. The pH was adjusted to 12 by adding NaCO while stirring and heating simultaneously. The hydrolysates were collected and inputted daily to four SBR reactors to augment nitrite shortcut for nitrogen depletion, using the real time aeration strategy suggested by Gao et al. (2009). The reactors were then operated for 35 days, during which parameters needed for further study were gathered for each scenario.

The organic waste, including SS, CM, FW, and CS, was first processed before being subjected to hydrolysis. The impact of the resulting hy- drolysates on nitrogen depletion was further performed in the sequential batch reactor (SBR) based in the inner wastewater treatment station of the plant. The wastewater was directly collected from the inlet of the station with a low C/N ratio (112.18 + 16.27 mg /69.1 + 4.9 mg TN.L-1. SS was collected at the treatment station's dewatering workshop; CM was collected from a chicken farm; FW (solid phase) was collected from a full-scale food waste treatment facility after selection and liquid/solid separation; CS was collected from the storage field of the nearby village.

The full processes were briefly described as follows: Four process lines representing SS, CM, FW, and CS scenarios were set up, respec- tively. The raw material was initially transported to pretreatment in each scenario. SS and CM were prepared by beads milling (magnetic stirring with grinding beads), whereas FW and CM were processed by high-pressure homogenization. The pretreated raw material was sub- jected to thermo-alkaline hydrolysis in a magnetically driven reactor. The pH was adjusted to 12 by adding NaCO while stirring and heating simultaneously. The hydrolysates were collected and inputted daily to four SBR reactors to augment nitrite shortcut for nitrogen depletion, using the real time aeration strategy suggested by Gao et al. (2009). The reactors were then operated for 35 days, during which parameters needed for further study were gathered for each scenario.
12: The elemental composition of raw materials was determined via a CHNS element analyzer (Elementar, Germany), whereas the biomass composition was realized by measuring the protein, lignin, hemicellu- lose, and cellulose using proximate method provided by Chinese Na- tional Standards (GB/T 212-2008). Further, the concentration of Chemical oxygen demands (COD), volatile fatty acids (VFA), NH3-N, NOx-N, total nitrogen (TN), total phosphorus (TP), and suspended solids (SS) were measured by conventional methods for the examination of water and wastewater. Particularly, total COD (TCOD) and soluble COD (SCOD) were distinguished. The total VFA composition was determined by chromatography (Agilent Technologies, US) equipped with a flame ionization detector, applying the operation parameters described by Chen and Chang (2017).
13: Total DNA was recovered from cell pellets collected from SCND units in each scenario during the stabilization phase. The SPINeasyTM DNA Kit (MP Biomedicals) was used to extract total genomic DNA samples. A Nano Drop ND-1000 spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) and agarose gel electrophoresis were used to assess the amount and quality of the extracted DNA. The primers 341F (5'-CCT ACG GGN GGC WGC AG-3') and 805R (5'-GAC TAC HVG GGT ATC TAA TCC-3') were used to amplify 16S rRNA. The 16S rRNA amplicons were sequenced using the Illumina Miseq platform. (Illumina, San Diego, California, USA). QIIME2 was then used to analyze the data for tax- onomical designations (Bolyen et al., 2019). The phylogenetic tree was built using FastTree based on the sequence alignment findings (Naushad et al., 2016); the metabolic function of the microbial community was predicted using the PICRUSt 2 program (Douglas et al., 2020). All sta- tistical analyses were carried out in R v3.6.1 (Team, 2013).
14: The fundamental goal of this study is to evaluate the environmental impacts of hydrolysates production and the stimulation of nitrogen depletion in wastewater, utilizing SS, CM, FW, and CS as raw materials. The trade-off between positive and negative environmental aspects is given special consideration. The system limits are cradle to gate, and all analyses followed the ISO principles and standards as proposed by updated research (Kohlheb et al., 2020). The findings of LCA may give references to relevant individuals and organizations in developing cost-effective management strategies for both organic waste and municipal wastewater while taking into account environmentally friendly approaches. Six units were built up, including transportation, storage, dozing, hydrolysis, and SCND. They were then combined in a full-scale LCA model, and the life cycle system boundary is shown in Fig. 1.
15: The LCA inventory was compiled using statistically averaged data collected during the experimental run (Table 1). The LCA models were based on the Chinese national dataset, and the assessment was per- formed by Gabi 8.5. (Khandelwal et al., 2019). A Monte Carlo simulation and sensitivity analysis were also performed to assess the accuracy of the LCA results and the influence of ore grade on environmental conse- quences. The functional unit was defined as the treatment of one m³ of wastewater to give an objective comparative reference for the evalua- tion of different objects (i.e. hydrolysates) under varied inventories, as well as to provide a quantitative reference for all associated inputs and outputs (Gallego-Schmid and Tarpani., 2019). The quantitative esti- mation of gas emissions was calculated based on guidelines published by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (Eggleston et al., 2006).
16: In this study, the CML 2001 approach was used as it provides a wide range of environmental mechanisms in comparison to other models (Dreyer et al., 2003). Eleven categories were selected to ensure comprehensive coverage in assessing the total environmental burden of the hydrolysis and SCND process, including sourced materials and en- ergy usage. Abiotic Depletion-Elements (ADP (elements)) is to analyze the resource depletion of a process with a unit of kg Sb equivalent (eq.); Acidification Potential (AP) measures the anthropocentric emissions of sulfur and nitrogen, as well as their oxides, with a unit of kg SO2 eq.; Eutrophication Potential (EP) is linked with the nutrients including ni- trogen and phosphorus release with a unit of kg phosphate eq.; Global Warming Potential (GWP) include the impact caused by greenhouse gas including CO2, N20, and CH4, etc., with a unit of kg CO2 eq.; Human Toxicity Potential (HTP) is environmental impact in case of toxicity, that is related to release of heavy metals, solvents, pesticides, etc., with a unit of kg 1, 2-dichlorobenzene (DCB) eq. Photochemical Ozone Creation Potential (POCP) refers to the compounds that can form ozone, with a unit of kg Ethane eq. The weighting analysis was further performed within the Gabi software to better reveal the environmental impacts of four scenarios. The sensitivity analysis was further carried out by reducing the specific factor (C/N ratio of the raw material in our case) by 5%, 10%, and 15%.
17: Four scenarios were subjected to an operational cost in this study. The operational cost is made up of internal and external costs. Internal costs were comprised of raw material, labor, power, auxiliary, and others such as tap water, sodium carbonate and additional chemicals with small amount, such as citric acid and ferric chloride (not considered for inventories). Raw material cost, power cost, and other cost were directly recorded and calculated on site. Labor cost was estimated based
Fig. 1. Schematic flowsheet and system boundaries of using hydrolysate of raw materials (SS,CM,FW,and CS) for SCND.
Table 1 Life cycle inventories for treating one m³ wastewater.

on the raw material input with a coefficient of 1.5 CNY/t per functional unit (one m³ wastewater); auxiliary cost were estimated based on a modified method provided by Ozdil and Tantekin (2016)., by excluding the purchase expenditure, according to auxiliary equipment of each scenario. External cost, on the other hand, mostly pertains to the control of gas pollutants during material digestion and subsequent SCND. They are output from inventories, including carbon dioxide (CO2), methane (CH4), dinitrogen oxides (NOx), ammonia (NH3), sulfur dioxide (SO2), and hydrogen sulfide (H2S). Using dose-response functions, the

environmental and health harm caused by gas pollutants has been analyzed, and a monetary price based on "willingness-to-pay" has been produced (Zhang et al., 2007). The external costs of these pollutants vary depending on studies. In this study, a widely accepted unit cost was used to calculate the external cost of each emission (Pa et al., 2013).

22: 3.1. Composition of raw materials and their hydrolysates

The composition of raw materials and their hydrolysates was investigated to provide details on the potential of organic waste hy- drolysates as a prospective stimulant for augmenting wastewater treat- ment by SCND. The elemental and biomass composition of the raw materials (SS, CM, FW, and CS) are presented in Fig. 2A, showing with a mass concentration (w/w) of dry weight. In general, all materials had high amount of carbon and oxygen content (25.6%-57.4% and 28.3% to 35.5, respectively), whereas the nitrogen and hydrogen content were at lower level (1.1%-5.5% and 3.1%-10.2%, respectively). Accordingly, the C/N ratio thereby can be calculated for SS, CM, FW, and CS (5.4, 19.7, 24.8, and 40.7 respectively). In anaerobic process, it was believed that complete degradation of organic waste requires C/N of 20-35, regardless, the optimum C/N ratio for hydrolysis is not recorded (Khalid et al., 2011). The four materials had different biomass compositions. SS was mainly composed of protein (31.3%), while CS had high amounts of cellulose (49.7%) and hemicellulose (26.1%). CM had an even distri- bution of organic composition, with approximately 14.4%, 16.2%,

10.7%, and 10.1% of protein, cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin, respectively. In case of FW, relatively higher amount of Protein and cellulose content (21.2% and 18.5%) were found in comparison with hemicellulose and lignin (7.7% and 9.6%).

Fig. 2B presents the TCOD, SCOD, and VFA concentrations of hy- drolysates. Overall, the TCOD, SCOD, and VFA concentration of four hydrolysates were in descending order as follow: FW > CM > CS > SS. In specific, the FW hydrolysate showed the highest biodegradability, as evidenced by its high TCOD (102.7 g/L), SCOD (48.4 g/L), and VFA (15.0 g/L) concentrations, in line with another study (Karthikeyan et al., 2016). In contrast, the SS hydrolysate had much lower TCOD (32.5 g/L), SCOD (5.7 g/L), and VFA (1.8g/L) concentrations, likely due to its lower organic carbon percentage compared to the other materials. This is mostly owing to sludge having a lower organic carbon percentage than other materials. After hydrolysis, on the other hand, there were still residual unbroken macromolecular compounds resulting in limited COD solubility and VFA production (Wang et al., 2016). Although the TCOD of CM and CS were similar (80.7 and 77.3 g/L, respectively), the SCOD and VFA of CM (32.6 and 21.9 g/L, respectively) were higher than those of CS (21.9 and 8.6 g/L, respectively), suggesting that the high cellulose and hemicellulose content in CS may have limited its hydrolysis

Fig. 2. Composition of raw materials (SS, CM, FW, and CS) and hydrolysates. (A) Element and biomass composition of raw materials; (B) TCOD, SCOD, and VFA concentration of hydrolysates. (C) VFA composition of hydrolysates.

efficiency. Fig. 2C shows the detailed VFA composition of hydrolysates. The listed VFA species accounted for 93%, 88%, 91%, and 85% of the total VFA. Diverse metabolic routes may coexist, moulding the VFA composition (Zhou et al., 2018). Our findings confirmed that similar VFA distribution patterns were observed across different raw materials. In this study, acetic acid was the dominating species in all hydrolysates of SS, CM, FW, and CS, respectively. In fact, acetic acid is usually the predominant product, implying robust reactor performance, and its production is considered favorable since acetic acid is a suitable low-molecular carbon and energy source that can be used by most mi- croorganisms for further nitrogen depletion in wastewater (Mamimin et al., 2015; Srisowmeya et al., 2020). Propionic acid, isobutyric acid, butyric acid, and isovaleric acid were also present in all hydrolysates, although their proportions varied. The concentrations of other acids were relatively low, except for a substantial amount of valeric acid discovered only in FW hydrolysate at a concentration of 1350 mg/L. The foregoing results suggest that hydrolysates from SS, CM, FW, and CS contain soluble organic carbon substrates including diverse species of VFA, serving as possible carbon and energy source stimulating further biological nitrogen depletion of wastewater in next stage.

10.7%, and 10.1% of protein, cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin, respectively. In case of FW, relatively higher amount of Protein and cellulose content (21.2% and 18.5%) were found in comparison with hemicellulose and lignin (7.7% and 9.6%).

Fig. 2B presents the TCOD, SCOD, and VFA concentrations of hy- drolysates. Overall, the TCOD, SCOD, and VFA concentration of four hydrolysates were in descending order as follow: FW > CM > CS > SS. In specific, the FW hydrolysate showed the highest biodegradability, as evidenced by its high TCOD (102.7 g/L), SCOD (48.4 g/L), and VFA (15.0 g/L) concentrations, in line with another study (Karthikeyan et al., 2016). In contrast, the SS hydrolysate had much lower TCOD (32.5 g/L), SCOD (5.7 g/L), and VFA (1.8g/L) concentrations, likely due to its lower organic carbon percentage compared to the other materials. This is mostly owing to sludge having a lower organic carbon percentage than other materials. After hydrolysis, on the other hand, there were still residual unbroken macromolecular compounds resulting in limited COD solubility and VFA production (Wang et al., 2016). Although the TCOD of CM and CS were similar (80.7 and 77.3 g/L, respectively), the SCOD and VFA of CM (32.6 and 21.9 g/L, respectively) were higher than those of CS (21.9 and 8.6 g/L, respectively), suggesting that the high cellulose and hemicellulose content in CS may have limited its hydrolysis

Fig. 2. Composition of raw materials (SS, CM, FW, and CS) and hydrolysates. (A) Element and biomass composition of raw materials; (B) TCOD, SCOD, and VFA concentration of hydrolysates. (C) VFA composition of hydrolysates.

efficiency. Fig. 2C shows the detailed VFA composition of hydrolysates. The listed VFA species accounted for 93%, 88%, 91%, and 85% of the total VFA. Diverse metabolic routes may coexist, moulding the VFA composition (Zhou et al., 2018). Our findings confirmed that similar VFA distribution patterns were observed across different raw materials. In this study, acetic acid was the dominating species in all hydrolysates of SS, CM, FW, and CS, respectively. In fact, acetic acid is usually the predominant product, implying robust reactor performance, and its production is considered favorable since acetic acid is a suitable low-molecular carbon and energy source that can be used by most mi- croorganisms for further nitrogen depletion in wastewater (Mamimin et al., 2015; Srisowmeya et al., 2020). Propionic acid, isobutyric acid, butyric acid, and isovaleric acid were also present in all hydrolysates, although their proportions varied. The concentrations of other acids were relatively low, except for a substantial amount of valeric acid discovered only in FW hydrolysate at a concentration of 1350 mg/L. The foregoing results suggest that hydrolysates from SS, CM, FW, and CS contain soluble organic carbon substrates including diverse species of VFA, serving as possible carbon and energy source stimulating further biological nitrogen depletion of wastewater in next stage.
27: 3.1. Composition of raw materials and their hydrolysates

The composition of raw materials and their hydrolysates was investigated to provide details on the potential of organic waste hy- drolysates as a prospective stimulant for augmenting wastewater treat- ment by SCND. The elemental and biomass composition of the raw materials (SS, CM, FW, and CS) are presented in Fig. 2A, showing with a mass concentration (w/w) of dry weight. In general, all materials had high amount of carbon and oxygen content (25.6%-57.4% and 28.3% to 35.5, respectively), whereas the nitrogen and hydrogen content were at lower level (1.1%-5.5% and 3.1%-10.2%, respectively). Accordingly, the C/N ratio thereby can be calculated for SS, CM, FW, and CS (5.4, 19.7, 24.8, and 40.7 respectively). In anaerobic process, it was believed that complete degradation of organic waste requires C/N of 20-35, regardless, the optimum C/N ratio for hydrolysis is not recorded (Khalid et al., 2011). The four materials had different biomass compositions. SS was mainly composed of protein (31.3%), while CS had high amounts of cellulose (49.7%) and hemicellulose (26.1%). CM had an even distri- bution of organic composition, with approximately 14.4%, 16.2%,

10.7%, and 10.1% of protein, cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin, respectively. In case of FW, relatively higher amount of Protein and cellulose content (21.2% and 18.5%) were found in comparison with hemicellulose and lignin (7.7% and 9.6%).

Fig. 2B presents the TCOD, SCOD, and VFA concentrations of hy- drolysates. Overall, the TCOD, SCOD, and VFA concentration of four hydrolysates were in descending order as follow: FW > CM > CS > SS. In specific, the FW hydrolysate showed the highest biodegradability, as evidenced by its high TCOD (102.7 g/L), SCOD (48.4 g/L), and VFA (15.0 g/L) concentrations, in line with another study (Karthikeyan et al., 2016). In contrast, the SS hydrolysate had much lower TCOD (32.5 g/L), SCOD (5.7 g/L), and VFA (1.8g/L) concentrations, likely due to its lower organic carbon percentage compared to the other materials. This is mostly owing to sludge having a lower organic carbon percentage than other materials. After hydrolysis, on the other hand, there were still residual unbroken macromolecular compounds resulting in limited COD solubility and VFA production (Wang et al., 2016). Although the TCOD of CM and CS were similar (80.7 and 77.3 g/L, respectively), the SCOD and VFA of CM (32.6 and 21.9 g/L, respectively) were higher than those of CS (21.9 and 8.6 g/L, respectively), suggesting that the high cellulose and hemicellulose content in CS may have limited its hydrolysis

efficiency. Fig. 2C shows the detailed VFA composition of hydrolysates. The listed VFA species accounted for 93%, 88%, 91%, and 85% of the total VFA. Diverse metabolic routes may coexist, moulding the VFA composition (Zhou et al., 2018). Our findings confirmed that similar VFA distribution patterns were observed across different raw materials. In this study, acetic acid was the dominating species in all hydrolysates of SS, CM, FW, and CS, respectively. 28: Wastewater was treated in a sequential batch reactor (SBR) unit with intermittent aeration and addition of hydrolysates for effective SCND. Loading ratio of hydrolysates in each unit was daily adjusted for SS, CM, FW, and CS, respectively to maintain the COD/TN ratio of ~6. To evaluate the stimulation effect of different hydrolysate on the biological nitrogen depletion in SCND process, the water quality of NH3-N, NOx-N, and TN were daily measured. Their values (influent and effluent) as a function of time are shown in Fig. 3. During the operational run, the pollutant loads for NH3-N, NOx-N, and TN ranged from approximately

44.3 to 58.7 mg/L per day (with a mean of 48.1 I 4.2), 0.3-2.5 mg/L per day (mean I sd: 14.0 I 1.9), and 54.1-76.7 mg/L per day (mean I sd: 69.1 I 4.9), respectively. The operations can be divided into two pha- ses: phase I (adaptation) and phase II (stabilization). The stabilized effluent parameters (phase II) were further compared to the legal re- quirements provided by Chinese urban sewage discharge 1A standard (GB18918-2002). It should be pointed out that the COD was also measured once per two days, but the data were not presented as the effluent concentration was consistently below the 50 mg/L limit estab- lished by the standard.

In phase I, four hydrolysates showed similar adaption effect until day 17, 10, 5, and 12 for SS, CM, FW, and CS, respectively, regarding the removal or accumulation of NH3-N, NOx-N, and TN until reaching sta- ble. In phase II, all hydrolysates showed a high removal efficiency for NH3-N throughout the stable period with a removal efficiency of over 90%. The SS, CM, FW, and CS supplemented units exhibited a fluctu- ating NH3-N effluent concentration of 0.73-7.32 (4.32 I 8.60), 0.18 to 8.29 (3.12 1 2.17), 0.44 to 8.55 (3.137 I 2.26), and 0.65 to 8.77 (4.16 I 2.78). These results all met the criteria of DB51/2311-2016, which specifies a qualified effluent with an NH3-N concentration of less than 5 mg/L. Importantly, the CM and FW hydrolysates showed slightly better performance than SS and CS hydrolysates with lower average NH3-N effluent concentration, shorter adaption phase, and fewer odd points exceeding the standard. In terms of NOx-N, the concentration in CM and FW hydrolysates supplemented effluents were low after adaption phase, ranging from 1.16 to 6.12 (3.3 + 1.4) and 0.43 to 6.32 (3.1 I 1.5) mg/L, respectively. Surprisingly, despite a high ratio of SCOD/TCOD, consid- erable accumulation of NOx-N was found in the effluent of CS hydroly- sate supplemented unit, comparable to that of SS hydrolysate, with effluent concentrations of 2.12-8.24 (5.9 I 1.3) and 4.65 to 8.77 (6.4 I 2.6) mg/L for SS and CS, respectively. Clearly, the overall nitrogen depletion in units supplemented with

Fig. 3. SCND performance as a function of time, supplemented with hydrolysate of raw materials (SS, CM, FW, and CS). (A-D) NH3-N removal; (E-H) NOx-N removal; (I-L) TN removal.

CM and FW hydrolysates was more efficient than that with SS and CS. This was further confirmed by the TN removal in phase II: utilizing hydrolysates, failure rates of TN removal were close to nil conforming the GB18918-2002 with an effluent concentration below 15 mg/L; TN removal rates using hydrolysates were better than those using organic waste directly applied, such as SS (60%-80%), CM (32%-80%), FW (74%), and CS (37%). The high VFA fraction in hydrolysates could be the reason for the more efficient biological nitrogen removal, even more SO than other commercial organics like ethanol and sodium acetate, as proven in previous studies (Puig et al., 2008; Yang et al., 2019).

The reduction of NOx-N is a heterotrophic process, which means the variation in biodegradable carbon concentrations in hydrolysates can affect denitrification efficiency. The better performance of CM and FW, in our study, may be attributed to the larger SCOD/TCOD ratio, indi- cating more degradable organic carbon was accessible for biological denitrification which was further supported by less accumulation of NOx-N. From another perspective, NH3-N oxidation is an autotrophic process, and the amount of biodegradable carbon barely affects its elimination. Moreover, the SS contained a large fraction of protein which may result in higher level of residual organic nitrogen, but also free ammonia during hydrolysis, inhibiting both nitrification and deni- trification process (Hiatt and Grady, 2008). In this case, it was reflected by the higher TN in the effluent of SS supplemented unit compared to others.

Surprisingly, despite a high ratio of SCOD/TCOD, consid- erable accumulation of NOx-N was found in the effluent of CS hydroly- sate supplemented unit, comparable to that of SS hydrolysate, with effluent concentrations of 2.12-8.24 (5.9 I 1.3) and 4.65 to 8.77 (6.4 I 2.6) mg/L for SS and CS, respectively. Clearly, the overall nitrogen depletion in units supplemented with

CM and FW hydrolysates was more efficient than that with SS and CS. This was further confirmed by the TN removal in phase II: utilizing hydrolysates, failure rates of TN removal were close to nil conforming the GB18918-2002 with an effluent concentration below 15 mg/L; TN removal rates using hydrolysates were better than those using organic waste directly applied, such as SS (60%-80%), CM (32%-80%), FW (74%), and CS (37%). The high VFA fraction in hydrolysates could be the reason for the more efficient biological nitrogen removal, even more SO than other commercial organics like ethanol and sodium acetate, as proven in previous studies (Puig et al., 2008; Yang et al., 2019).

The reduction of NOx-N is a heterotrophic process, which means the variation in biodegradable carbon concentrations in hydrolysates can affect denitrification efficiency. The better performance of CM and FW, in our study, may be attributed to the larger SCOD/TCOD ratio, indi- cating more degradable organic carbon was accessible for biological denitrification which was further supported by less accumulation of NOx-N. From another perspective, NH3-N oxidation is an autotrophic process, and the amount of biodegradable carbon barely affects its elimination. Moreover, the SS contained a large fraction of protein which may result in higher level of residual organic nitrogen, but also free ammonia during hydrolysis, inhibiting both nitrification and deni- trification process (Hiatt and Grady, 2008). In this case, it was reflected by the higher TN in the effluent of SS supplemented unit compared to others.
32: Wastewater was treated in a sequential batch reactor (SBR) unit with intermittent aeration and addition of hydrolysates for effective SCND. Loading ratio of hydrolysates in each unit was daily adjusted for SS, CM, FW, and CS, respectively to maintain the COD/TN ratio of ~6. To evaluate the stimulation effect of different hydrolysate on the biological nitrogen depletion in SCND process, the water quality of NH3-N, NOx-N, and TN were daily measured. Their values (influent and effluent) as a function of time are shown in Fig. 3. During the operational run, the pollutant loads for NH3-N, NOx-N, and TN ranged from approximately

44.3 to 58.7 mg/L per day (with a mean of 48.1 I 4.2), 0.3-2.5 mg/L per day (mean I sd: 14.0 I 1.9), and 54.1-76.7 mg/L per day (mean I sd: 69.1 I 4.9), respectively. The operations can be divided into two pha- ses: phase I (adaptation) and phase II (stabilization). The stabilized effluent parameters (phase II) were further compared to the legal re- quirements provided by Chinese urban sewage discharge 1A standard (GB18918-2002). It should be pointed out that the COD was also measured once per two days, but the data were not presented as the effluent concentration was consistently below the 50 mg/L limit estab- lished by the standard.

In phase I, four hydrolysates showed similar adaption effect until day 17, 10, 5, and 12 for SS, CM, FW, and CS, respectively, regarding the removal or accumulation of NH3-N, NOx-N, and TN until reaching sta- ble. In phase II, all hydrolysates showed a high removal efficiency for NH3-N throughout the stable period with a removal efficiency of over 90%. The SS, CM, FW, and CS supplemented units exhibited a fluctu- ating NH3-N effluent concentration of 0.73-7.32 (4.32 I 8.60), 0.18 to 8.29 (3.12 1 2.17), 0.44 to 8.55 (3.137 I 2.26), and 0.65 to 8.77 (4.16 I 2.78). These results all met the criteria of DB51/2311-2016, which specifies a qualified effluent with an NH3-N concentration of less than 5 mg/L. Importantly, the CM and FW hydrolysates showed slightly better performance than SS and CS hydrolysates with lower average NH3-N effluent concentration, shorter adaption phase, and fewer odd points exceeding the standard. In terms of NOx-N, the concentration in CM and FW hydrolysates supplemented effluents were low after adaption phase, ranging from 1.16 to 6.12 (3.3 + 1.4) and 0.43 to 6.32 (3.1 I 1.5) mg/L, respectively. Clearly, the overall nitrogen depletion in units supplemented with

CM and FW hydrolysates was more efficient than that with SS and CS. This was further confirmed by the TN removal in phase II: utilizing hydrolysates, failure rates of TN removal were close to nil conforming the GB18918-2002 with an effluent concentration below 15 mg/L; TN removal rates using hydrolysates were better than those using organic waste directly applied, such as SS (60%-80%), CM (32%-80%), FW (74%), and CS (37%). The high VFA fraction in hydrolysates could be the reason for the more efficient biological nitrogen removal, even more SO than other commercial organics like ethanol and sodium acetate, as proven in previous studies (Puig et al., 2008; Yang et al., 2019).

The reduction of NOx-N is a heterotrophic process, which means the variation in biodegradable carbon concentrations in hydrolysates can affect denitrification efficiency. The better performance of CM and FW, in our study, may be attributed to the larger SCOD/TCOD ratio, indi- cating more degradable organic carbon was accessible for biological denitrification which was further supported by less accumulation of NOx-N. From another perspective, NH3-N oxidation is an autotrophic process, and the amount of biodegradable carbon barely affects its elimination. Moreover, the SS contained a large fraction of protein which may result in higher level of residual organic nitrogen, but also free ammonia during hydrolysis, inhibiting both nitrification and deni- trification process (Hiatt and Grady, 2008). In this case, it was reflected by the higher TN in the effluent of SS supplemented unit compared to others.
36: Hydrolysates have a significant impact on the composition of bac- teria, which are essential to the nitrogen removal system (Qiu et al., 2021). The microbial profiles and their essential functions in each SCND unit were analyzed to better understand the variations among four hy- drolysates affecting the microbial community. Fig. 4A, B, and 4C illus- trate the distribution of microbial structure during SCND treatment when using SS, CM, FW, and CS hydrolysates to treat wastewater. The dominating bacteria at phylum level were Proteobacteria, followed by Firmicutes, Myxococcota, Acidobacteriota, Bacteroidota, and Nitro- spirota in all units applying different hydrolysates.

Proteobacteria is a phylum of bacteria commonly found in biological wastewater treatment processes, and includes a variety of ammonia oxidizers that form a nitrite loop with nitrifiers (Yang et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2019). Notably, Proteobacteria was more abundant (41.0%) in the unit supplemented with FW hydrolysate than that with SS (28.7%), CM (25.6%), and CS (28.6%) hydrolysate. Alternatively, the other domi- nating phyla did not show significant differences in terms of abundance in all units. Further, Gammaproteobacteria, belonging to Proteobac- teria, was the most frequently identified bacteria in all units at class level, followed by Alphaproteobacteria, Clostridia, Bacteroidia, and Vicinamibacteria. Gammaproteobacteria were revealed to have mixo- trophic nitrifying-denitrifying metabolic pathways in addition to being

Fig. 4. Metagenomic analysis of SCND unit supplemented with hydrolysate of raw materials (SS, CM, FW, and CS). (A) Microbial composition at phylum level; (B) Microbial composition at class level; (C) Phylogenetic tree at genus level; (D) Key functions.

important denitrifying bacteria (Liu et al., 2020; Mori et al., 2019). Again, Gammaproteobacteria was the richest class (30.2%) in FW hy- drolysate supplemented unit compared to the others. In addition, Clos- tridia also showed significantly higher abundance (10.8%) in FW hydrolysate supplemented unit compared to that in others. It is also worth noting that Nitrospira markedly appeared (1.2%-3.8%) in all units, which were frequently identified as nitrite-oxidizing bacteria (Bayer et al., 2021). Interestingly, Alphaproteobacteria and Bacteroidia were discovered relatively more abundant in SS and CS supplemented units, when compared to the other two units.

To the genus level, different patterns of distribution were presented in four units. Dubosiella (5.6%) and Methanosaeta (4.1%) were the most abundant genera in FW hydrolysate supplemented unit, followed by Enterobacter (2.3%), SWB02 (2.0%), and Nitrospiraceae (1.6%). It is worth noting that, Enterobacter displayed concurrent aerobic denitrifi- cation and nitrification (Padhi et al., 2017), whereas Nitrospiraceae (Liao et al., 2018) play a critical role in nitrification belonging to Nitrospira. The leading genera, regarding SS hydrolysate supplemented unit, were Arcobacter (2.9%) and Denitratisoma (3.2%) which were both important denitrifiers within the shortcut pathway (Pishgar et al., 2019; Wu et al.,

2020). These two genera were also frequently identified in CM hydro- lysate supplemented unit (2.4% and 1.9%, respectively), together with Kouleothrix (1.6%) and SM1A02 (1.7%). Moreover, Arcobacter was the leading genus in CS supplemented unit, with an abundance of 3.2%, whereas significant Comamonas (1.5%) was also discovered.

Fig. 4D shows the metabolic functions predicted via PICTURUS software, and those of nitrogen metabolisms were presented, classified as denitrification, nitrification and ammonification. Denitrification was displayed by the sum of genes of nar, nir, nor, and nos, encoding nitrate reductase, nitrite reductase, nitric oxide reductase, nitric oxide reduc- tase, and nitrous oxide reductase, whereas nitrification was subjected to the sum of genes of amo and nxr, encoding ammonia monooxygenase and nitrite oxidoreductase (Philippot et al., 2001; Chen et al., 2018). In addition, ammonification, during which residual organic nitrogen was decomposed to NH3-N, was shown by genes of nrf, encoding pentaheme cytochrome C nitrite reductase (Simon, 2002). It is noted that the highest abundance of denitrification (0.09% + 0.02%) and nitrification (0.01% + 0.003%) appeared in FW hydrolysate supplemented unit. This is consistent with the above results in nitrogen removal efficiency, indi- cating that FW was the best candidate among others, stimulating

Fig. 5. Environmental impact for treating one m3 wastewater. (A) Midpoint results of Abiotic Depletion-Elements (ADP (elements)); (B) Midpoint results of Acidification Potential (AP); (C) Midpoint results of Photochemical Ozone Creation Potential (POCP); (D) Midpoint results of Eutrophication.

Further, the lowest abundance of denitrification was found in SS scenario, however, that of nitrification was found in CS scenario. Remarkably, the highest abundance of ammonification was found in CM, regardless, with high variation. This did not impair with the assumption that effluent of SS hydrolysate supplemented unit harbored higher amount of residual organic nitrogen, thereby in which ammo- nification should be more abundant. In fact, these discrepancies may be caused by bias of inaccurate prediction on ammonification due to generally low abundance. Overall, the above results indicate that bac- teria with key functions for nitrogen depletion were widely detected in all units, with the most abundant being found in the FW hydrolysate supplemented unit. However, it should be pointed out that, indeed, the presence of other undiscussed bacteria so as to their functions were still difficult to explain.

The dominating bacteria at phylum level were Proteobacteria, followed by Firmicutes, Myxococcota, Acidobacteriota, Bacteroidota, and Nitro- spirota in all units applying different hydrolysates.

Proteobacteria is a phylum of bacteria commonly found in biological wastewater treatment processes, and includes a variety of ammonia oxidizers that form a nitrite loop with nitrifiers (Yang et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2019). Notably, Proteobacteria was more abundant (41.0%) in the unit supplemented with FW hydrolysate than that with SS (28.7%), CM (25.6%), and CS (28.6%) hydrolysate. Alternatively, the other domi- nating phyla did not show significant differences in terms of abundance in all units. Further, Gammaproteobacteria, belonging to Proteobac- teria, was the most frequently identified bacteria in all units at class level, followed by Alphaproteobacteria, Clostridia, Bacteroidia, and Vicinamibacteria. Gammaproteobacteria were revealed to have mixo- trophic nitrifying-denitrifying metabolic pathways in addition to being

Fig. 4. Metagenomic analysis of SCND unit supplemented with hydrolysate of raw materials (SS, CM, FW, and CS). (A) Microbial composition at phylum level; (B) Microbial composition at class level; (C) Phylogenetic tree at genus level; (D) Key functions.

important denitrifying bacteria (Liu et al., 2020; Mori et al., 2019). Again, Gammaproteobacteria was the richest class (30.2%) in FW hy- drolysate supplemented unit compared to the others. In addition, Clos- tridia also showed significantly higher abundance (10.8%) in FW hydrolysate supplemented unit compared to that in others. It is also worth noting that Nitrospira markedly appeared (1.2%-3.8%) in all units, which were frequently identified as nitrite-oxidizing bacteria (Bayer et al., 2021). Interestingly, Alphaproteobacteria and Bacteroidia were discovered relatively more abundant in SS and CS supplemented units, when compared to the other two units.

To the genus level, different patterns of distribution were presented in four units. Dubosiella (5.6%) and Methanosaeta (4.1%) were the most abundant genera in FW hydrolysate supplemented unit, followed by Enterobacter (2.3%), SWB02 (2.0%), and Nitrospiraceae (1.6%). It is worth noting that, Enterobacter displayed concurrent aerobic denitrifi- cation and nitrification (Padhi et al., 2017), whereas Nitrospiraceae (Liao et al., 2018) play a critical role in nitrification belonging to Nitrospira. The leading genera, regarding SS hydrolysate supplemented unit, were Arcobacter (2.9%) and Denitratisoma (3.2%) which were both important denitrifiers within the shortcut pathway (Pishgar et al., 2019; Wu et al.,

2020). These two genera were also frequently identified in CM hydro- lysate supplemented unit (2.4% and 1.9%, respectively), together with Kouleothrix (1.6%) and SM1A02 (1.7%). Moreover, Arcobacter was the leading genus in CS supplemented unit, with an abundance of 3.2%, whereas significant Comamonas (1.5%) was also discovered.

Fig. 4D shows the metabolic functions predicted via PICTURUS software, and those of nitrogen metabolisms were presented, classified as denitrification, nitrification and ammonification. Denitrification was displayed by the sum of genes of nar, nir, nor, and nos, encoding nitrate reductase, nitrite reductase, nitric oxide reductase, nitric oxide reduc- tase, and nitrous oxide reductase, whereas nitrification was subjected to the sum of genes of amo and nxr, encoding ammonia monooxygenase and nitrite oxidoreductase (Philippot et al., 2001; Chen et al., 2018). In addition, ammonification, during which residual organic nitrogen was decomposed to NH3-N, was shown by genes of nrf, encoding pentaheme cytochrome C nitrite reductase (Simon, 2002). It is noted that the highest abundance of denitrification (0.09% + 0.02%) and nitrification (0.01% + 0.003%) appeared in FW hydrolysate supplemented unit. This is consistent with the above results in nitrogen removal efficiency, indi- cating that FW was the best candidate among others, stimulating

Fig. 5. Environmental impact for treating one m3 wastewater. (A) Midpoint results of Abiotic Depletion-Elements (ADP (elements)); (B) Midpoint results of Acidification Potential (AP); (C) Midpoint results of Photochemical Ozone Creation Potential (POCP); (D) Midpoint results of Eutrophication.

Further, the lowest abundance of denitrification was found in SS scenario, however, that of nitrification was found in CS scenario. Remarkably, the highest abundance of ammonification was found in CM, regardless, with high variation. This did not impair with the assumption that effluent of SS hydrolysate supplemented unit harbored higher amount of residual organic nitrogen, thereby in which ammo- nification should be more abundant. In fact, these discrepancies may be caused by bias of inaccurate prediction on ammonification due to generally low abundance. Overall, the above results indicate that bac- teria with key functions for nitrogen depletion were widely detected in all units, with the most abundant being found in the FW hydrolysate supplemented unit. However, it should be pointed out that, indeed, the presence of other undiscussed bacteria so as to their functions were still difficult to explain.

44: The use of waste organic hydrolysates significantly improved the nitrogen depletion efficiency, providing environmental benefits. How- ever, the production of hydrolyaste and SCND process require additional inputs that result in environmental burdens. In order to understand the trade-offs involved in using the four materials for nitrogen removal in wastewater, a life cycle environmental impact analysis was carried out, and the midpoint results were presented in Fig. 5A-F. The midpoint result of SS on ADP (6.04E-10 kg Sb eq.) and POCP (3.35E-04 kg Ethene eq.) was the lowest among four materials, but relatively high for AP (1.35E-03 kg SO2 eq.) and HTP (2.19E-03 DCB eq.). On the contrary, FW had a highest result of ADP (1.40E-09 kg Sb eq.) and POCP (6.38E- 04 kg Ethene eq.), but a relatively lower result of AP (3.40E-04) and HTP (2.15E-04).

Furthermore, FW and CS had the lowest AP and HTP results, but also highest ADP and GWP results. Prominently, the midpoint results of all categories on CM were significant. The differences in impact categories among scenarios may be explained by differences in electrical input, which led to varying amounts of atmospheric sulfur emissions, with the exception of EP, which was considered to have low influence by elec- tricity (Piao et al., 2016; Rodriguez-Garcia et al., 2011). Positive results for ADP, AP, GWP, HTP, and POCP indicate negative impacts, while negative results for EP indicate positive impacts. However, both CM and FW had the same EP value (-0.0427 kg Phosphate eq.), which was significantly higher than that of SS and CS (-0.0395 and -0.0391 kg Phosphate eq.). Commercial carbons, however, were characterized with negative impact on EP when added to wastewater treatment because nutrients leakage in production does not compensate the nitrogen removal (Rodriguez-Garcia et al., 2014).

Weighting was used in this study to evaluate the prominent mid- points. It provides a single score analysis of various materials throughout hydrolysis and SCND using a cradle-to-gate approach, entailing com- parison of above environmental impact categories at the same dimen- sion. The weighted values of all impact categories were shown in Fig. 5G. It is noted that the impacts on EP and GWP made the greatest contribution, representing the environmental benefit and burden respectively.

In particular, the weighted environmental impact score on EP was extraordinarily high in all scenarios. The EP was directly associated with the corresponding nitrogen removal performance, which is in line with Piao's results, but in contrary to Rodriguez-Garcia (Piao et al., 2016; Rodriguez-Garcia et al., 2011). Typically, EP is expected to decrease from the point at which the effluent was formed in the upstream units. In this case, we computed the net result while excluding the negative environmental impact in terms of EP created by the process line itself as a consequence of, for example, electricity and diesel usage (Godin et al., 2012). The weighted score of GWP was equally high, on the same scale of magnitude as that of EP. It was reported that external carbon source can result in more than 30% of the total environmental impact

(Rodriguez-Garcia et al., 2014). The primary cause of high GWP was direct gas emissions from hydrolysate production and SCND, particu- larly CH4 and N2O, constituting significant concern as there were yet no commonly accepted solutions for their effective management (Rashid and Liu, 2020). Finally, the effects of AP and ADP were minimal, whereas that of the HTP was negligible.

The environmental benefits reflected by the negative score of EP were significantly larger than the total of positive scores from the remaining environmental burden categories for all scenarios, which were reflected by net environment benefit (NEB) that were calculated as follows:
Where i and j are the environmental impact categories; WEIPOS refers to weighted environmental impact with positive score; WEIneg refers to weighted environmental impact with negative score.

The life cycle impact analysis did not take C/N ratio of the raw materials into account. In fact, when C/N ratio decreases, the power and material consumption necessary to sustain adequate microbial metab- olitic activity rises significantly which further increases the environ- mental burden. Thus, we investigated the effect of C/N ratio on all environmental categories (Table 2) in four scenarios. Results showed that a 5% decrease in C/N ratio had a slight effect on the environmental impact in the CM and FW scenarios across all categories. However, in the SS scenario, the decrease in EP with a 5% decrease in C/N ratio was significant, with a decrease of 9.11%. When C/N ratio decreased by 10% and 15%, the effect on environmental impacts became substantial. From another perspective, the sensitivity analysis showed the most significant impact on EP for all scenarios. In specific, the environmental impact on EP decreased by 10.10%-35.44% and 38.11%-61.01% at these tow variations, respectively. Further, a 10% decrease in C/N ratio resulted in an increase of 6.62%-29.88% in GWP, while a 15% decrease led to an increase of 29.88%-44.30% in GWP. As the other impact categories were insignificant, thus were not further interpreted and discussed.

The differences in impact categories among scenarios may be explained by differences in electrical input, which led to varying amounts of atmospheric sulfur emissions, with the exception of EP, which was considered to have low influence by elec- tricity (Piao et al., 2016; Rodriguez-Garcia et al., 2011). Positive results for ADP, AP, GWP, HTP, and POCP indicate negative impacts, while negative results for EP indicate positive impacts. However, both CM and FW had the same EP value (-0.0427 kg Phosphate eq.), which was significantly higher than that of SS and CS (-0.0395 and -0.0391 kg Phosphate eq.). Commercial carbons, however, were characterized with negative impact on EP when added to wastewater treatment because nutrients leakage in production does not compensate the nitrogen removal (Rodriguez-Garcia et al., 2014).

Weighting was used in this study to evaluate the prominent mid- points. It provides a single score analysis of various materials throughout hydrolysis and SCND using a cradle-to-gate approach, entailing com- parison of above environmental impact categories at the same dimen- sion. The weighted values of all impact categories were shown in Fig. 5G. It is noted that the impacts on EP and GWP made the greatest contribution, representing the environmental benefit and burden respectively.

In particular, the weighted environmental impact score on EP was extraordinarily high in all scenarios. The EP was directly associated with the corresponding nitrogen removal performance, which is in line with Piao's results, but in contrary to Rodriguez-Garcia (Piao et al., 2016; Rodriguez-Garcia et al., 2011). Typically, EP is expected to decrease from the point at which the effluent was formed in the upstream units. In this case, we computed the net result while excluding the negative environmental impact in terms of EP created by the process line itself as a consequence of, for example, electricity and diesel usage (Godin et al., 2012). The weighted score of GWP was equally high, on the same scale of magnitude as that of EP. It was reported that external carbon source can result in more than 30% of the total environmental impact

(Rodriguez-Garcia et al., 2014). The primary cause of high GWP was direct gas emissions from hydrolysate production and SCND, particu- larly CH4 and N2O, constituting significant concern as there were yet no commonly accepted solutions for their effective management (Rashid and Liu, 2020). Finally, the effects of AP and ADP were minimal, whereas that of the HTP was negligible.

The environmental benefits reflected by the negative score of EP were significantly larger than the total of positive scores from the remaining environmental burden categories for all scenarios, which were reflected by net environment benefit (NEB) that were calculated as follows:
Where i and j are the environmental impact categories; WEIPOS refers to weighted environmental impact with positive score; WEIneg refers to weighted environmental impact with negative score.

The life cycle impact analysis did not take C/N ratio of the raw materials into account. In fact, when C/N ratio decreases, the power and material consumption necessary to sustain adequate microbial metab- olitic activity rises significantly which further increases the environ- mental burden. Thus, we investigated the effect of C/N ratio on all environmental categories (Table 2) in four scenarios. Results showed that a 5% decrease in C/N ratio had a slight effect on the environmental impact in the CM and FW scenarios across all categories. However, in the SS scenario, the decrease in EP with a 5% decrease in C/N ratio was significant, with a decrease of 9.11%. When C/N ratio decreased by 10% and 15%, the effect on environmental impacts became substantial. From another perspective, the sensitivity analysis showed the most significant impact on EP for all scenarios. In specific, the environmental impact on EP decreased by 10.10%-35.44% and 38.11%-61.01% at these tow variations, respectively. Further, a 10% decrease in C/N ratio resulted in an increase of 6.62%-29.88% in GWP, while a 15% decrease led to an increase of 29.88%-44.30% in GWP. As the other impact categories were insignificant, thus were not further interpreted and discussed.

50: Cost analysis necessitates a thorough grasp of the whole process operation and provides hints for detecting significant environmental implications. It is believed that when LCA is used for process compari- son, discussion only with the operating phase is sufficient; when the treatment process is with a complicated system or built underground, considering the construction and demolition phases is necessary (Li et al., 2017; Polruang et al., 2018; Zhang, 2016). In this study, the capital cost including construction and demolition was not covered in this study, because they have far less impact than the operational pa- rameters (Li et al., 2017; Polruang et al., 2018).
The operational cost shares the same system boundaries with LCA, while it consists of internal cost and external cost. The following equa- tion was used for calculation:

Where OPC stands for the operational cost; CIn,i refers to the internal cost of each cost category; Ej refers to the external costs per unit of gas pollutant j; and ej stands for the quantity of each emission category.

Table 3 depicts the distribution of operational costs within system boundaries, which includes both internal and external costs. Overall, the total cost for treating one m³ wastewater in SS and CS scenarios were equivalent (0.508 and 0.535 CNY, respectively), whereas that in CM and FW scenarios were at the same level as well (0.911 and 0.864 CNY, respectively). Internal cost for four scenarios followed a similar pattern to the total cost, with the internal cost of the CM and FW scenarios being
Table 2 Sensitivity analysis of decreasing C/N ratio of raw materials on the life cycle environmental impacts.
Operational cost analysis for treating one m³ wastewater.

Category|SS|CM|FW|CS Internal cost (CNY)|Internal cost (CNY)|Internal cost (CNY)|Internal cost (CNY)|Internal cost (CNY) Raw material|7.29E-02|4.88E-01|3.45E-01|2.58E-01 Labor|2.27E-02|8.61E-03|6.83E-03|9.33E-03 Power|1.28E-01|1.08E-01|7.18E-02|9.82E-02 Auxiliary|1.53E-01|1.32E-01|1.99E-01|9.55E-02 Others|8.58E-02|1.27E-01|9.61E-02|2.90E-02 SUM(Internal)|4.62E-01|8.64E-01|7.19E-01|4.90E-01 External cost (CNY)|External cost (CNY)|External cost (CNY)|External cost (CNY)|External cost (CNY) Carbon dioxide (CO2)|2.51E-03|3.17E-03|2.08E-03|2.28E-03 Methane (CH4)|1.65E-02|2.55E-02|3.61E-02|2.03E-02 Dinitrogen oxides(NOx)|1.34E-02|8.26E-03|3.80E-03|1.90E-03 Dinitrogen monoxide (N20)|4.44E-05|3.84E-05|4.21E-05|5.47E-05 Ammonia (NH3)|5.38E-03|7.64E-04|9.68E-02|1.40E-02 Sulfur dioxide (SO2)|5.61E-03|8.14E-03|6.28E-03|6.10E-03 Hydrogen sulfide (H2S)|1.50E-03|1.85E-03|2.90E-04|2.90E-04 SUM (External)|4.51E-02|4.76E-02|1.45E-01|4.49E-02 Total cost|5.08E-01|9.11E-01|8.64E-01|5.35E-01
approximately 46.7%-87.0% higher than the SS and CS scenarios. The raw material cost was mainly generated from the transportation and pretreatment of SS, CM, FW, and CS.

In our case, the most prominent internal cost was raw material cost except for SS scenario. The raw material cost of SS scenario only accounted for 0.0729 CNY (15.7% of the internal cost), as it was directly collected on site. The CM scenario had the highest raw material cost of 0.488 CNY (56.5% of internal cost), followed by the FW and CS sce- narios with raw material costs of 0.345 and 0.258 CNY (48.0% and 53.7% of internal cost), respectively. Furthermore, the cost of auxiliary ranked the second place ranging from 0.0955 to 0.153 CNY for all sce- narios, particularly, that of CS was at lowest. Power costs were likewise considerable for all scenarios, ranging from 0.0718 to 0.128 CNY. The costs were calculated based on a unit cost coefficient of 0.80 MJ/CNY for electricity and 0.23 MJ/CNY for diesel (Yang et al., 2021). Because only the materials addition was considered, the labor cost was the lowest internal cost. However, it is necessary to point out that, if subjected to a centralized WWTP, labor cost should be elevated and monthly paid as more intensive work. In terms of other cost, the CS was relatively low compared to the others. The sum of external cost only accounted for 5.2%-16.4% of total cost for treating one m³ of wastewater, whereas the highest external cost referred to the CM scenario. For all scenarios, the external cost of managing methane was fairly high in comparison to other pollutants.

In terms of other cost, the CS was relatively low compared to the others. The sum of external cost only accounted for 5.2%-16.4% of total cost for treating one m³ of wastewater, whereas the highest external cost referred to the CM scenario. For all scenarios, the external cost of managing methane was fairly high in comparison to other pollutants.

In this study, the capital cost including construction and demolition was not covered in this study, because they have far less impact than the operational pa- rameters (Li et al., 2017; Polruang et al., 2018).
