58: The use of organic waste hydrolysate for SCND in wastewater is an emerging area of research that has the potential to significantly improve the sustainability and efficiency of wastewater treatment. However, to fully realize the potential of this approach and to ensure consistent and high-quality hydrolysate production, a standardized process for feed- stock selection and pretreatment is necessary. This process should involve selecting the appropriate feedstock based on its organic matter content, nutrient composition, and potential contaminants. Proper pre- treatment of the feedstock is also crucial, as this will ensure that the feedstock is properly prepared for hydrolysis. The use of a standardized process will not only help keep the quality of the hydrolysate produced, but will also provide a foundation for future studies and applications that aim to use organic waste hydrolysate for nitrogen depletion in wastewater. On the other hand, regular testing and quality control measures are also essential to validate the process is proceeding as ex- pected, and that any necessary corrective actions are taken in a timely manner.

This study provides credible qualitative research that illustrates the potential benefits of using organic waste hydrolysate for nitrogen depletion in wastewater applications. These results can be generalized to other studies and future applications that adopt a similar technological scheme, offering a scientific basis for assessing tradeoffs among per- formance, costs, and environmental burdens. By building on the findings of this study, further work can develop and refine technologies for uti- lizing organic waste hydrolysate to enhance nitrogen removal in wastewater, with confidence that the approach is scientifically sound and can be applied reliably in different contexts. Thus, more efficient and environmentally friendly organic waste management and waste- water treatment practices can be achieved, promoting a more sustain- able future.
59: The use of organic waste hydrolysate for SCND in wastewater is an emerging area of research that has the potential to significantly improve the sustainability and efficiency of wastewater treatment. However, to fully realize the potential of this approach and to ensure consistent and high-quality hydrolysate production, a standardized process for feed- stock selection and pretreatment is necessary. This process should involve selecting the appropriate feedstock based on its organic matter content, nutrient composition, and potential contaminants. Proper pre- treatment of the feedstock is also crucial, as this will ensure that the feedstock is properly prepared for hydrolysis. The use of a standardized process will not only help keep the quality of the hydrolysate produced, but will also provide a foundation for future studies and applications that aim to use organic waste hydrolysate for nitrogen depletion in wastewater. On the other hand, regular testing and quality control measures are also essential to validate the process is proceeding as ex- pected, and that any necessary corrective actions are taken in a timely manner.

This study provides credible qualitative research that illustrates the potential benefits of using organic waste hydrolysate for nitrogen depletion in wastewater applications. These results can be generalized to other studies and future applications that adopt a similar technological scheme, offering a scientific basis for assessing tradeoffs among per- formance, costs, and environmental burdens. By building on the findings of this study, further work can develop and refine technologies for uti- lizing organic waste hydrolysate to enhance nitrogen removal in wastewater, with confidence that the approach is scientifically sound and can be applied reliably in different contexts. Thus, more efficient and environmentally friendly organic waste management and waste- water treatment practices can be achieved, promoting a more sustain- able future.
60: Sustainable management of organic wastes considers both environ- mental prevention and resource recycling. In this study, we produced hydrolysates from organic wastes and successfully applied them as a novel stimulant augmenting shortcut initrification-denitrification (SCND) for biological nitrogen depletion in wastewater treatment. The results showed that the composition of hydrolysates was carbon-rich, containing various VFA species that are favorable carbon sources for microbial proliferation. Further, we demonstrated that nitrogen removal rate and microbial functions were successfully improved by hydroly- sates, with CM and FW hydrolysate showing the highest performance. Life cycle assessment finally showed that, the environmental benefit resulting from negative eutrophication potential covered all
environmental burdens indicating the overall process is profitable.

Our findings indicate that organic waste hydrolysates have a high potential for use in wastewater treatment; nevertheless, problems about a future scale-up industrial application must be addressed. For example, centralized WWTP has unsteady influent and more complex treatment systems. When all relevant system boundaries (e.g., sludge disposal) are considered, the input/output inventories may greatly vary in cases, leading to different results from treatment performance to environ- mental impact evaluation. Besides, the collection of organic waste is another issue that should be considered for determining whether in-situ and ex-situ hydrolysis is required. Finally, for feasibility purposes, the life cost analysis should be compared to alternative approaches for recycling organic waste, such as producing fertilizer, fuels, and pesti- cides, etc.
